steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: Training a model is cooking the meal. Serving it is running the restaurant. A great recipe (model) means nothing if orders take 30 minutes (high latency) or the kitchen burns down under rush hour (no scaling).

steps.label: How It Works
steps.body:
Common serving stacks on Linode GPU instances:

- **vLLM** — high-throughput LLM serving with PagedAttention, OpenAI-compatible API
- **Text Generation Inference (TGI)** — Hugging Face's optimized server, supports quantized models
- **Triton Inference Server** — NVIDIA's multi-framework server (ONNX, TensorRT, PyTorch)
- **Ollama** — simple local LLM runner, good for prototyping

All run in Docker containers with NVIDIA GPU passthrough.

steps.label: Real World
steps.body: Quick start with vLLM: `docker run --gpus all -p 8000:8000 vllm/vllm-openai --model Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct`. This gives you an OpenAI-compatible API in one command. For production, add a NodeBalancer in front of multiple GPU instances for load balancing and health checks.
"vLLM for LLMs, TGI for Hugging Face models, Triton for multi-framework. All run on GPU Linodes with Docker + NVIDIA Container Toolkit."

quiz.question: You need an OpenAI-compatible API for a 7B parameter LLM. What do you use?

options.explain: You'd serve a pre-trained model, not train one.

options.text: vLLM with the --model flag pointing to a Hugging Face model
options.explain: vLLM provides an OpenAI-compatible API out of the box. Point it at any supported model.

options.text: Host the model on Object Storage
options.explain: Object Storage stores files — it doesn't serve inference.
EdgeWorkers run lightweight JS, not GPU inference workloads.

quiz.question: How do you scale inference across multiple GPU Linodes?

options.text: Run one giant model across all GPUs via MPI
options.explain: That's distributed training, not serving. For serving, each instance runs independently.

options.text: NodeBalancer in front of multiple GPU instances, each running the same model
options.explain: Horizontal scaling: each GPU Linode serves the same model, NodeBalancer distributes requests.
You absolutely can scale horizontally with a load balancer.

options.text: Use Kubernetes HPA on CPU metrics
options.explain: GPU workloads need GPU-aware scaling, not CPU-based HPA.
